headlineSummary: Discussion skewed negative for Microsoft Copilot on limited evidence, while OpenAI was also criticized, Anthropic drew mixed reactions, and Gemini had a small positive lean [E3] [E1] [E2].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot was criticized in a strongly negative comment that blamed Microsoft’s Copilot push for broader product and QA problems, but this rests on limited evidence [E3].
OpenAI had a negative comment about Codex causing a mistaken git revert that erased accumulated work, but this also rests on limited evidence [E1].
Anthropic’s Claude had mixed feedback: one comment praised it for regex and shell one-liners, while others said it still produces buggy code or ignores tools [E2] [E4] [E5] [E6].
Google Gemini got a small positive mention for being reliable at schema and syntax generation, but the evidence is limited [E2].

evidence.excerpt: > The only other notable setback was an accidental use of the word "revert" which Codex took literally, and ran git revert on a file where 1-2 hours of progress had been accumulating.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Codex and describes it causing a harmful mistaken action that lost accumulated work.

evidence.excerpt: do SoTA models like Opus, Gemini and friends actually need output schema enforcement still, or has all the the RLVR training they do on generating code and json etc. made schema errors vanishingly unlikely? Because as a user of those models, they almost never make syntax mistakes in generating json and code

annotations.rationale: Opus is an Anthropic model-family alias, and the commenter says these models almost never make syntax mistakes.
Gemini is named directly, and the commenter praises the models' schema/syntax reliability.

evidence.excerpt: Instead of putting Copilot everywhere, Microsoft could hire some proper devs and qa to avoid such problems, But no, MS employs vibe-coders only and fired the entire qa team.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly blames Microsoft's push to put Copilot everywhere for neglecting engineering and QA, using strongly critical language.

evidence.excerpt: I have seen colleagues get excited about using Claude to write their software for them, and then end up spending at least as much time as if they had written it themselves trying to develop a theory the code that was produced, and an understanding sufficient to correct the problems and bugs in the created code.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names Claude and says using it does not save time because users still must understand and fix buggy generated code.

evidence.excerpt: I'm using Claude (Opus 4.5 via Code) every day and it's very good with regexes, sed, awk and similar bash oneliners.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly praises Claude's performance on regexes and shell one-liners.

evidence.excerpt: I have chunckhound is a few projects and it’s noted in both the agent md file as well as mcp and claude never uses it. Ever. Never once.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names claude and complains that it never uses the provided tool integration.
